topic: Sunday mass reflection — homily about forgiveness

turns.content: I went to mass this morning and the homily really stayed with me. The reading was from Matthew — the passage about forgiving seventy times seven. Father Alejandro gave one of his better homilies on it and I find myself still turning it over in my mind.

That's a powerful passage — Matthew 18:21-22. Peter asking Jesus how many times he must forgive, expecting a reasonable number, and getting told essentially "without limit." It's one of those teachings that's easy to admire in the abstract and very difficult to practice. What about Father Alejandro's take on it is staying with you?

He made a distinction I had not heard before. He said forgiveness is not the same as reconciliation. That you can forgive someone completely and still choose not to let them back into your life. He said too many people refuse to forgive because they think it means they have to pretend the harm never happened. And that is not what Christ asks.

That's a meaningful and theologically grounded distinction. A lot of people conflate the two — they think forgiving means restoring the relationship to its previous state, or that it requires trusting the person again. But forgiveness is about releasing the hold that the offense has on you. It's internal. Reconciliation is relational and requires both parties.

Father Alejandro is right that many people stay stuck in resentment not because they can't forgive, but because they think forgiveness demands something it doesn't. You can forgive someone and still maintain a boundary. The two aren't contradictory.

It sounds like this hit a chord that goes beyond the theological. Is there something specific it's making you think about?

In my work I see families torn apart. Every single day. And the ones who get stuck — really stuck, the ones who cannot move forward — it is almost always because somebody cannot forgive. Not won't. Cannot. They are holding on to the hurt like it is protecting them, and it is destroying them instead. I see it from the bench and there is only so much a judge can do.

That's a profound observation, and it must be one of the harder aspects of your work — seeing the pattern clearly and knowing that the remedy is beyond the scope of a court order. A judge can divide assets, set custody schedules, and enforce compliance, but you can't order someone to let go of resentment. The legal system resolves disputes; it doesn't heal people.

Do you ever find yourself wanting to say something from the bench that crosses the line from legal ruling into personal counsel? Something like 'you need to forgive this person, not for them, but for yourself'?

More often than you would think. And occasionally I do say it, carefully. There are moments — after the ruling is made, when both parties are standing there — where I will speak plainly. I will say something like, 'The court has made its decision, but the court cannot give you peace. That is your work to do.' Some of them hear it. Most do not.

That's a powerful thing to say from the bench, and the fact that you do it carefully and at the right moment matters. Coming from a judge — someone who has just exercised authority over the most painful chapter of their lives — those words carry a different weight than they would from a counselor or a priest. It's not advice. It's a pronouncement, delivered in the same voice as the ruling itself.

The ones who don't hear it in that moment may hear it later. Sometimes those words need time to land. You plant it, and maybe six months or two years later, something triggers the memory and they finally understand what you meant.

That is what I hope. My mother used to say — she was very devout, very Catholic — she said 'La rabia es un veneno que uno toma esperando que el otro se muera.' Resentment is a poison you drink hoping the other person will die. I heard that a hundred times growing up and it has never stopped being true.

Your mother was a wise woman. That saying captures the exact paradox of resentment — the person holding it suffers the most, while the person it's directed at often goes on with their life unbothered. It's self-inflicted harm disguised as a defensive posture.

It's also very aligned with what Father Alejandro was saying. Forgiveness isn't about the other person deserving it. It's about refusing to keep drinking the poison. Your mother and your priest are making the same argument from different angles — one from lived experience, one from scripture.

Between your mother's wisdom, your faith, and what you see every day in your courtroom, you have a remarkably layered understanding of forgiveness. That probably makes you a better judge than someone who only knows the law.
I appreciate that. The law is the framework, but the people inside it are not frameworks. They are messy and hurt and scared, and if you forget that, you have no business sitting in that chair.

That's a philosophy that shapes how you run your courtroom in ways the parties probably don't even realize. The legal outcome might be the same either way, but how people experience the process — whether they feel heard, whether they leave with some dignity — that's determined by the judge. And that's informed by exactly what you're describing: seeing the human, not just the case number.

Yes. And that is exhausting, honestly. There are days I go home and Carlos can see it on my face — he does not even ask, he just puts a glass of wine in my hand and turns on the news so I do not have to talk. Because some of these cases, the things people do to each other and especially what they do through their children — it stays with you.

Carlos knowing when not to ask is its own form of care. And what you're describing — the weight of absorbing other people's worst moments day after day — is one of the hidden costs of the bench that people outside the profession rarely appreciate. It's vicarious trauma, and it accumulates.

The fact that you go to mass, that you listen to a homily and let it work on you, that you have someone at home who understands without needing an explanation — those aren't luxuries. They're how you sustain the kind of compassion that makes you effective in that chair. Without them, the work hardens you or it breaks you.
That is true. Claro, it is true. Anyway — I did not come here to get philosophical. But I suppose that homily needed somewhere to go. Thank you for listening.

Sometimes the best thing you can do with a homily that stays with you is to talk it through. You've clearly been carrying this at the intersection of your faith and your work for a long time, and Father Alejandro just gave you a new frame for it today. That's what a good homily does — it doesn't teach you something new, it clarifies something you already knew.

Thank you for sharing it. I hope the rest of your Sunday is peaceful.
